coredata.dc:title: Synchronized sensing and annihilation of virulent organo-contaminants in water bodies using a highly fluorescent novel nanohybrid of Tulsi (Ocimum Tenuiflorum) leaves derived carbon dots and NiFe2O4 nanoparticles

coredata.dc:description: Tulsi Leaves were used to fabricate highly fluorescent carbon dots (TCDs), which were evenly anchored on the surface of NiFe2O4 (NF) through facile hydrothermal route to design smart magnetically retrievable fluorescent nanohybrids NFTCDs, not reported hitherto. NFTCDs initially assess the quantity of hazardous contaminants in aqueous samples and then eradicate them through photo-Fenton degradation mechanism. The fluorescent probe displayed ultra-trace detection limit towards PNP (65 nM), DNP (74 nM), TNP (78 nM), MET (57 nM), and DCNA (100 nM) with superior photocatalytic removal efficiencies (>86%) in comparison to pristine NF nanoparticles. Further, the smart platform based on NFTCDs presented satisfactory reliabilities and accuracies for quantification of baneful toxins in real samples with recyclability up to four cycles. Thus, this work provides a novel NFTCDs nanohybrid which can be well-used to design an intelligent platform to synchronize the monitoring and extirpation of the deleterious contaminants for human safety.

head.ce:title: Synchronized sensing and annihilation of virulent organo-contaminants in water bodies using a highly fluorescent novel nanohybrid of Tulsi (Ocimum Tenuiflorum) leaves derived carbon dots and NiFe2O4 nanoparticles

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tulsi Leaves were used to fabricate highly fluorescent carbon dots (TCDs), which were evenly anchored on the surface of NiFe2O4 (NF) through facile hydrothermal route to design smart magnetically retrievable fluorescent nanohybrids NFTCDs, not reported hitherto. NFTCDs initially assess the quantity of hazardous contaminants in aqueous samples and then eradicate them through photo-Fenton degradation mechanism. The fluorescent probe displayed ultra-trace detection limit towards PNP (65 nM), DNP (74 nM), TNP (78 nM), MET (57 nM), and DCNA (100 nM) with superior photocatalytic removal efficiencies (>86%) in comparison to pristine NF nanoparticles. Further, the smart platform based on NFTCDs presented satisfactory reliabilities and accuracies for quantification of baneful toxins in real samples with recyclability up to four cycles. Thus, this work provides a novel NFTCDs nanohybrid which can be well-used to design an intelligent platform to synchronize the monitoring and extirpation of the deleterious contaminants for human safety.

An intelligent nanohybrid was contrived through assimilation of highly fluorescent green Tulsi leaves derived carbon dots and stellar magnetic photocatalyst NiFe2O4 for the methodical photoluminescence based detection and photocatalytic eradication of nitroexplosives (PNP, DNP, and TNP), antibiotic (MET), and pesticide (DCNA).
Unlabelled Image

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Rapid population growth and consequent amplification of industrial-agricultural activities across the globe bequeath significant stress by virtue of emitting huge amounts of adulterants into the environment. Recent studies have shown that release of these emerging pollutants have breached the earth's threshold for chemical pollution [1,2]. Nitrophenols (nitroexplosives) belong to one of the largely produced chemical intermediates and explosive materials engender from a variety of industrial sources associated with petrochemical units, oil refineries, polymeric resin manufacturing, and plastic units. However, nitroexplosives (NEs) such as 4-nitrophenol (PNP), 1,3-dinitrophenol (DNP), and 1,3,5-trinitrophenol (TNP) are listed as precedence toxic chemical with limitation of discharge below 0.5 mg/L imposed by the present legislation by the USEPA because of their huge toxicity, mutagenicity, and carcinogenicity [3]. By the same token, pesticides and antibiotics are widely used in agriculture and aquaculture to raise agricultural output and treat bacterial infections. However, in recent decades, the abuse of pesticides and antibiotics has induced high levels of residue in both plants and animals, which can be ultimately ingested by humans through food chain. Metronidazole (MET) is a synthetic nitroimidazole-based drug with high antibiotic and anticoccidial activities but cases such as metronidazole overdose or mass metronidazole poisoning in clinic are frequently encountered and therefore its use in animal feed has been banned [4]. Similarly, 2,6-dichloro-4-nitroaniline (DCNA or dicloran) is one of the widely used pesticides for protecting crops from various diseases but at the same time, DCNA belongs to the toxic pesticide of grade IV and may cause serious damage to ecological security thus intimidating human health [5]. These venomous chemicals possess high resistance rates, non-biodegradability and high solubility in water due to which these are difficult to eradicate and can outlast under environmental condition. Such persistent noxious chemicals are highly carcinogenic and can cause mutation, deformity, immense damage to human organs and immune system [2]. Therefore, preliminary step to safeguard the environment is to develop perspicacious strategy which not only identifies such ‘invisible killers’ but also annihilate them.

Identification and eradication of the noxious pollutants have utmost significance for the sustainable advancement but coinciding the two features i.e., recognition and elimination at one platform largely remain a challenge to scientific community [6]. Fluorescence sensing and photodegradation based methods have been considered as promising technologies in the detection and extermination of venomous contaminants because of many advantages such as fast and easy operation coupled with energy efficient mechanism [6]. Carbon dots (CDs) are noble carbon nanomaterials which possess unique properties such as ease of synthesis and functionalization, chemical stability, strong photostability, water solubility, and biocompatibility [7,8]. In recent years majority of reports have stressed upon the significance of highly fluorescent CDs in the detection of pestilent chemicals, however, due to water solubility of CDs, most of the reports do not describe the sensor's recyclability. The problem can be addressed by incorporating magnetic properties into CDs, which not only allow magnetic recycling behavior but also cut the system's cost and thereby benefit in protecting water resources with minimal risks of secondary contamination. In the same context, Liu et al. designed a low cost chemosensor by integrating strong fluorescence property of CDs and magnetism of amino-functionalized magnetic core-shell nanomaterial Fe@SiO2 NH2 for the sensitive and selective determination of 2, 4, 6-trinitrotolune [9]. The sensor Fe@SiO2 NH2 also possessed excellent reusability up to 10 consecutive cycles. Similarly, Wang's group developed magnetic fluorescent sensor for bisphenol A by adopting one-pot synthesis strategy based on magnetic covalent organic framework-supported, CD-embedded molecular imprinted composite (MCOF@CD@MIP). This sensor also exhibits fast recycling behavior for consecutive 5 cycles [10]. Furthermore, the application range of magnetic CDs can be extended by employing them for the degradation of toxic compounds. Recently, Kaur et al. prepared Fe3O4-CDs for the Fenton type degradation of toxic doxycycline drug [11]. The catalyst Fe3O4-CDs also exhibits excellent magnetic recyclability and reusability. Likewise, Wu and co-workers fabricated flower like FeOOH hybridized with CDs for efficient photo-Fenton degradation of organic pollutants accompanied by magnetic recycling performance up to 5 cycles [12]. Although most of the literature reports described that the employed magnetic CDs can perform sensing or degradation application one at a time but to date very few reports are available which can show potential of magnetic CDs for simultaneous detection and degradation of pollutants. On another side, magnetic NiFe2O4 (NF) nanoparticles (NPs) are the extensively used heterogeneous Fenton catalyst which can obliterate the noxious pollutants through photocatalytic degradation [13,14]. Yet the reported studies are exiguous in meeting the primary requirement of environmental protection i.e., identification of ‘invisible killers’ using NF NPs. Despite of great potential, scarce reports are available on simultaneous fluorescent detection and photocatalytic removal of virulent waste utilizing remarkable abilities of CDs and NF NPs. Therefore, it is desirable to develop a smart magnetic platform based on CDs and NF that enables the dual function of fluorescence detection and photodegradation at a single location.

With these motivations, herein, a novel nanohybrid NFTCDs was innovatively designed that comprise of green source (Krishna Tulsi leaves) derived CDs (TCDs) and magnetic NF NPs, which served as multifunctional material to achieve simultaneous fluorescence detection and photocatalysis of pernicious organic compounds. The TCDs were readily prepared via one-pot hydrothermal method using Krishna Tulsi leaves as low-cost green precursor. The fabricated NFTCDs nanohybrid not only surpassed the restrictions of CDs in PL sensing but also exhibited benefits of photocatalytic degradation and magnetic recyclability of the catalyst. Successful formation of pristine TCDs, pristine NF NPs and NFTCDs nanohybrid was confirmed by different techniques and finally the detection and removal efficiencies of NFTCDs nanohybrid were analyzed using NEs (PNP, DNP, TNP), antibiotic (MET), and pesticide (DCNA) as model pollutants. Besides, the potential mechanisms of PL detection and photo-Fenton catalysis towards the virulent chemicals were also explored along with real-time monitoring. The biocompatible nature and toxicant free attributes of treated water was further ensured by phytotoxicity study. Admirable results demonstrated that the NFTCDs nanohybrid can aid in designing of multifunctional fluorescent-photocatalyst coupled with magnetic field assisted recoverability of catalyst to simultaneously detect and remove deleterious organic pollutants in the aqueous environment.

All general reagents and solvents were of AR grade and used directly without any anterior purification. Ferric nitrate (Fe(NO3)2·9H2O), nickel nitrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), disodium hydrogen orthophosphate anhydrous (Na2HPO4), sodium dihydrogen orthophosphate monohydrate (NaH2PO4·H2O), PNP, DNP, TNP, and DCNA were acquired from CDH. 0.1 M aqueous solution of phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was prepared by adding the calculated amount of Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4·H2O to give PBS of desired pH. pH of all the solutions was configured using 0.1 M HCl and 0.1 M NaOH. Metrogyl injection (containing 5 mg/mL of MET) was purchased from local chemist shop and used to study the analysis of MET antibiotic. For the interference studies, all the chemicals were purchased from Avra Synthesis Pvt. Ltd. All the solutions were prepared in double-distilled water obtained from the ultrafiltration system milli-Q system.

Physicochemical properties and morphological features of the synthesized materials were explored using different analytical techniques. Phase structure and phase purity was studied by X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Panalytical's X'Pert Pro diffractometer), at a scan rate of 1° min−1 in the 2θ range of 10° to 80° with Cu Kα radiation. Raman spectroscopy was performed on STR 500 confocal RAMAN spectrometer with 532 nm excitation laser wavelength. Functional groups present in the synthesized materials were examined by recording fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra in the range of 4000–400 cm−1 on Perkin Elmer 400 FT-IR spectrophotometer. PHI 5000 VersaProbe III was used to measure X-ray photoelectron Spectra (XPS). The microstructures and morphologies of the materials were determined by collecting field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) images on Hitachi-SU8010 and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images on FEI Tecnai (G2 F20). Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns attached with HR-TEM instrument was used for the elemental composition analysis and identification of crystallinity, respectively. All the fluorescence experiments were carried out on Hitachi fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000) at room temperature. Fluorescence lifetime measurements were recorded using Horiba Flex with LED pulsed excitation source of 325 nm. All the UV–visible spectra were obtained using JASCO (Model V-750) instrument. The magnetic behavior was investigated by vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) using MICROSENSE EZ7 at room temperature. The quantification of metal ions (Fe and Ni) leached to the reaction solution was carried out on Agilent 7900 inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometer (ICP-MS) instrument.

The highly fluorescent TCDs were synthesized by an accessible hydrothermal method using holy Krishna Tulsi leaves (Ocimum Tenuiflorum) as carbon source (Scheme 1 ). In a typical method, 10 g of freshly obtained Tulsi leaves were pulverized into 100 mL of double distilled water with the help of household grinder and then transferred to a 150 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for different time intervals (2–6 h) in an electric oven. After cooling to room temperature, the products were centrifuged and the supernatant fraction was filtered again using Whatman 42 filter paper to obtain a clear yellow-brown solution. Further, the solution was purified with a dialysis bag (MWCO: 3000 K Da) for 15 h and finally freeze-dried to form brown solid. The collected TCDs were stored under 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of magnetic NFTCDs nanohybrid and pristine NF NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Magnetic NFTCDs containing different weight percentages (5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%) of NF were synthesized via a facile one pot hydrothermal route (Scheme 1). Briefly, 5 mmol of Ni(NO2)3.6H2O and 10 mmol of Fe(NO2)3.9H2O were mixed in minimum amount of double distilled water followed by addition of liq. NH3 dropwise until pH 7.5 was attained. Subsequently, certain amount of synthesized TCDs (mg) were added to the solution of metal nitrates and magnetically agitated for 2 h. Finally, the reaction mixtures were transferred to a 150 mL capacity Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 15 h. After natural cooling to ambient temperature, a brown NFTCDs suspension was filtered and washed with water-ethanol mixture for several times followed by drying at 70 °C in hot air oven. In this work, the as prepared nanohybrids were labelled as xNFTCD, where x = 5, 10, 15, and 20 wt% of NF. Pristine NF NPs were also synthesized by following the same methodology except the addition of TCDs.

In a typical experimental procedure, 10 mg of finely grounded NFTCDs were dispersed in 10 mL of double distilled water to form a uniform suspension (1 mg/mL) with ultrasonic treatment for 1 h. All fluorescence titration experiments were performed by gradually adding aqueous solution of nitroanalytes (0.1 mM) and drugs (1 mM), whereas, the pesticides dissolved in acetonitrile (0.1 mM) were used for assessment. Fluorescence detections were accomplished at emission wavelength (λem) ranging from 330 nm to 630 nm with an excitation wavelength (λex) of 320 nm and slit width of 10 nm at room temperature. For each PL measurement, 1 cm width quartz cuvette was used for the test. While executing each fluorescence titration, the dispersion was incessantly agitated to maintain the homogeneity. All the experiments were performed thrice to ensure reproducibility of the results.

All the photocatalytic experiments were conducted under optimized conditions (100 mL of solution contaminated with 0.50 mM of pollutant, [H2O2] = 8.8 mM, 0.50 g/L of photocatalyst) at room temperature. A 400 W Hg lamp operating at 220 V was used as artificial source of visible light illumination. Photocatalytic degradation of NEs (PNP, DNP, and TNP), antibiotic (MET), and pesticide (DCNA) were studied using synthesized photocatalyst (NFTCDs nanohybrid and pristine NF). pH is the important parameter in photodegradation using Fenton process and it is well established in the literature that the Fenton reactions occurred favourably in the acidic pH range (2–3) due to generation of surplus •OH radicals in this pH range [6,15]. Therefore, all the degradation reactions were performed at fixed pH = 2.5. In a typical procedure, desired amount of catalyst was dispersed in the contaminated solution through ultra-sonication and then subsequently stirred under dark conditions for 30 min to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium. A required amount of H2O2 was then added to the reaction mixture and immediately subjected to light source. A fixed aliquot of the reaction suspension was taken out at fixed interval of time and the residual concentration of the contaminant was determined using UV–vis spectrophotometer.

Methi (Trigonella foenum-graecum) seeds were used to evaluate the phytotoxicity of NFTCDs nanohybrid and contaminated water before and after photo-treatment with NFTCDs nanohybrid. Test seeds were sterilized with 10% sodium hypochlorite solution and then thoroughly rinsed with water. Healthy and uniform sized seeds were selected and swamped for 2 h under different sets of conditions: (i) River water (control), (ii) NFTCDs solution of concentration 50 mg/mL, (iii) contaminated (with PNP, DNP, TNP, MET, and DCNA) river water before photo-treatment with NFTCDs, and (iv) contaminated (with PNP, DNP, TNP, MET, and DCNA) river water after photo-treatment with NFTCDs. After 2 h, the seeds were dried and incubated for 10 days on moistened tissue paper with 10 seeds in each petri dish. The experiments were performed thrice. After 10 days, the length of seedlings was measured and different attributes to measure the phytotoxicity such as germination percentage (GP) and seedling vigour index (GI) was calculated using Eq. (1) and Eq. (2), respectively;
(1)
GP =
Germinated seeds / total number of seeds
× 100
(2)
SVI = GP ×
average length of the seedling

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of TCDs, NF NPs and NFTCDs nanohybrid
Structural characterization: powder-XRD, Raman spectroscopy, FTIR and XPS analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Structural characteristics and phase purity of the synthesized materials were studied through powder XRD measurements. Fig. 1 displays the XRD patterns obtained for pristine NF NPs, pristine TCDs, and xNFTCDs (x = 5, 10, 15, and 20 wt% of NF) nanohybrids. The diffraction pattern of all NFTCDs nanohybrids and pristine NF NPs, exhibited characteristic peaks at 2θ = 30.25° [220], 35.50° [311], 43.28° [400], 52.50° [422], 58.10° [511], 63.80° [440], 71.30° [533], 73.01° [622], and 76.90° [444], which confirmed the formation of pure cubic structure of NF NPs with Fd3m space group (JCPDS No. 00–110-0325) [3]. Additionally, a diffraction hump at ∼20°-25° in the diffraction pattern of pure TCDs was observed which is a characteristic of CDs and corresponded to 002 facets (d spacing value 0.320 nm) of graphite structure [16]. Also, the emergence of this hump in diffraction pattern of all NFTCDs nanohybrids further confirmed the successful insertion of TCDs onto NF surface resulting into magnetic as well as fluorescent nanohybrid. Moreover, absence of any additional peaks assured the purity of the synthesized materials.

Raman spectroscopy is an effective way to assess the state of carbon in a carbonaceous material. Fig. 2 displays typical Raman spectra of pristine NF, pristine TCDs, and 10NFTCDs nanohybrid. As illustrated in Fig. 2, spectra of pristine NF and 10NFTCDs showed peaks in the range of 400–800 cm−1 which were distinctive feature of spinel type structure and its existence thereby confirmed the formation of NF NPs [3,17]. The Eg mode at ∼390 cm−1 belonged to symmetric, whereas T2g mode at ∼540 cm−1 belonged to asymmetric bending of oxygen anions with Fe/Ni metal cations. Moreover, the A1g mode at ∼698 cm−1 could be ascribed to the symmetric vibrational stretching of oxygen anions along NiO and FeO bonds. In addition, Raman spectra of pristine TCDs and 10NFTCDs found to exhibit two characteristic peaks: a D band at ∼1370 cm−1 and a G band at ∼1570 cm−1. The D band reflected the disordered sp3 carbon, whereas, the G band originated due to in-plane vibrations of sp2 carbon which represented the crystallinity/graphitization of the material [18]. The relative intensity of D-band to G-band (ID/ IG) can be used to assess the degree of structural defects. The ID/IG value for TCDs and 10NFTCDs were 0.54 and 0.89, respectively. Both the values are <1, indicating high degree of graphitization. However, higher value of ID/IG for 10NFTCDs indicated that TCDs with massive defected structure were attached on the surface of NF NPs, further enhancing the defects. Thus, confirming the successful formation of NF NPs decorated with TCDs.

To further gain the insight of molecular interactions occurring between the surface functional groups, FTIR studies were performed. Fig. S1 shows the FT-IR spectra for pristine NF, pristine TCDs, and xNFTCDs (x = 5, 10, 15, and 20 wt% of NF) nanohybrids. The FT-IR spectra of NF revealed a characteristic strong absorption band of ferrite structure at ∼585 cm−1 corresponding to intrinsic stretching vibrations of metal‑oxygen bond (Ni/FeO) [3]. Additionally, a prominent band at 3391 cm−1 and a weak peak at 1385 cm−1 were observed, which could be assigned to stretching and bending vibrations of O ̶ H group, respectively, originated due to adsorbed surface water molecules. However, the peaks at 1611 cm−1 and 1052 cm−1 corresponded to asymmetric vibrations of CO3 2−, appeared due to the impurities from the reaction precursors. Further, the spectra of all NFTCDs nanohybrid and pristine TCDs showed characteristic peaks which were ascribed as follows: 3391 cm−1 (OH stretching of hydroxyl group), 3226 cm−1 (NH stretching of amine groups), 2838 cm−1 (CH stretching of aliphatic carbon), 1611 cm−1 (CO stretching of carbonyl group), 1385 cm−1 (OH deformation), and 1052 cm−1 (CO deformation) [16,19]. Also, the appearance of distinctive absorption band corresponding to Ni/FeO bond stretching vibration in spectra of all NFTCDs nanohybrids, confirmed the presence of NF in NFTCDs nanohybrids, substantiating their performance further.

XPS was further used to elucidate the specific bonding and chemical states of the elements in pristine NF NPs, pristine TCDs, and 10NFTCDs nanohybrid (Fig. 3 ). The full survey scan XPS spectrum of pristine TCDs displayed the existence of C 1s (284.0 eV), N 1s (398.0 eV), and O 1s (530.0 eV) (Fig. 3(a)). The high resolution spectrum of C 1s demonstrated five distinct peaks centred at 281.6 eV, 282.4 eV, 283.5 eV, 284.8 eV, and 285.9 eV which confirmed the presence of CC, COH/C ̶ H, C ̶ OX, C=O/O ̶ C ̶ O, and C(=O)OX bonds in synthesized TCDs (Fig. 3(b)) [20]. High-resolution XPS spectra of the N 1s band was deconvoluted into two components related to C ̶ N and N ̶ H groups with binding energies at 397.0 eV and 398.6 eV, respectively (Fig. S2) [20,21]. The detailed O 1s XPS spectrum presented two deconvoluted peaks at 527.8 eV and 529.6 eV, which were attributed to the C ̶ OH/C ̶ O ̶ C and CO groups, respectively (Fig. S2). Moreover, the wide survey scan of pristine NF identified the presence of O 1s (∼530.0 eV), Fe 2p (∼715.0 eV), and Ni 2p (∼850.0 eV) (Fig. 3(a)) [3,17]. The Fe 2p spectrum in Fig. 3(c) was fitted to two peaks at 709.1 eV and 721.9 eV, linked to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 spin orbital components, respectively along with a satellite peak for Fe 2p3/2. The deconvoluted XPS peaks of Ni 2p were centred at the binding energies 852.9 eV and 870.9 eV and were assigned to the Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2, respectively, along with a satellite peak of Ni 2p3/2 (Fig. 3d). Thus, confirming the formation of NF. The high-resolution spectrum of O 1s could be deconvoluted into two peaks. Additionally, Ni/FeO signal appeared at 529.7 eV, whereas the other peak at 531.1 eV was due to chemisorbed and dissociated oxygen species, respectively (Fig. S2). The full range XPS spectra of NFTCDs nanohybrid consists of chemical compositions (C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, Fe 2p and Ni 2p) which were characteristics of pristine NF NPs and TCDs (Fig. 3(a)). A noticeable increase in the peak intensity of C=O/OCO bond (Fig. 3(b)) and increase in binding energy of COH/OCO bonds from 527.8 eV to 529.2 eV in 10NFTCDs hybrid (Fig. (S2)), confirmed the successful formation of NFTCDs nanohybrid cluster through the interaction between C=O/OCO and Ni/FeO bonds (Fig. 3(c-d)). Further, the surface of synthesized 10NFTCDs composed of multiple functional groups attached to C framework which resulted in excellent hydrophilicity and dispersibility in aqueous medium for subsequent application in sensing.

FE-SEM was employed to examine the surface morphology and microstructure of the as-synthesized pristine TCDs and magnetic 10NFTCD hybrid clusters (Fig. 4 ). The FE-SEM images exhibited (Fig. 4(a)) spherical morphology and uniform distribution of TCDs with an average particle size of 40 ± 5 nm. At higher temperature tiny particles of CDs stacked to form larger particles, apparently looking bigger in size [22]. Moreover, 10NFTCD hybrid clusters obtained by a hydrothermal self-assembly method were evidently spherical and ∼ 200 nm in size as shown in Fig. 4(b). Surface of the hybrid cluster was found to be uneven which could be entitled as ‘stuffed globule’, as the TCDs were clearly seen embedded on the surface of NF, to bring forward the porous structure of the hybrid, confirming the successful formation of NFTCD hybrid clusters.

Further, the extremely small sizes of TCDs exceeds the resolution of FE-SEM (1–20 nm) whereas, HR-TEM exhibits a much higher resolving power (∼0.2 nm), therefore, it was used to analyze the structure and lattice imperfections of TCDs and their nanohybrids with NF [22]. Fig. 4(c, d) and Fig. 4(g, h) unveiled the TEM micrographs of TCDs and 10NFTCDs nanohybrid, respectively, at low and high magnification. Pristine TCDs exhibited well distributed spherical particles in the range of 1–8 nm with an average size of 3.29 nm and d-spacing of 0.21 nm which matched well to 002 crystal plane of graphite structure (Fig. 4(d, f)). Similarly, Fig. 4(h, j) displayed the aggregated spherical particles of 10NFTCDs with average particle size of 282 nm and d-spacing (0.25 nm) corresponded to (311) plane of NF. In addition, Fig. 4(g, h) clearly displayed a large number of tiny spherical TCDs of sizes ∼2 nm embodied within the porous matrix of NF NPs. Due to high molecular mass of NF, these particles were appeared darker however, CDs being lesser in weight, found to be lighter in appearance and thus unequivocally confirmed the successful formation of 10NFTCDs nanohybrid. These outcomes were in well consistency with the FE-SEM results. Besides, Fig. 4(e, i) demonstrated the SAED patterns of pristine TCDs (indexed with (002)) and 10NFTCDs (indexed with (002), (311), and (440)) which corroborated well with the XRD results and thus further confirmed their successful formation [3]. Elemental analysis was also carried out by EDS attached with HRTEM which revealed the existence of envisaged elements i.e., C, N and O in pristine TCDs whereas C, N, O, Fe and Ni in synthesized 10NFTCDs nanohybrid (Fig. S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties, magnetic properties, and phytotoxicity assessment of synthesized NFTCDs
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the photo-physical properties of NFTCDs, UV–vis and PL spectra were recorded by means of UV–vis, PL spectrophotometer, and UV–vis DRS. As shown in inset of Fig. 5(a), aqueous suspension of 10NFTCDs displayed light brown colour under natural day light, however, it illuminated luminous green colour upon excitation under UV source of 365 nm wavelength. This could be explained as follows: UV–vis absorbance spectrum of 10NFTCDs aqueous solution displayed two prominent excitonic absorption bands located ∼220 nm and 290 nm, besides an absorption edge at ∼450 nm. The eminent absorption band below 300 nm could be attributed to π-π* transitions within the sp2 carbon core of conjugated skeleton. Whereas, the weak absorption shoulder could be ascribed to the heteroatom-doped surface state or the energy trap caused by n-π* (nO2p-π* and nN2p-π*) transitions of carboxyl/carbonyl/amine functional groups [23,24]. Moreover, the PL emission spectra of 10NFTCDs dispersion showed excitation wavelength (λex) maxima at 320 nm and the corresponding emission wavelength (λem) maxima at 430 nm, signifying green fluorescence of 10NFTCDs aqueous suspension. Further, the band gap of all the synthesized samples was calculated using Tauc plot as shown in Fig. S4. The band gaps for pristine NF, pristine TCD, and 10NFTCD were estimated to be 1.55 eV, 1.61 eV, and 1.50 eV, respectively. Inclusion of TCDs decreased the band energy gap of NF due to the presence of sub-band energy level between TCD and NF. Formation of sub-band energy level arise because of the π conjugated TCDs, where TCDs could act as photosensitizer to introduce more photogenerated electrons to the conduction band (CB) of NF. The photosensitizing ability could be due to the plentiful oxygen-containing groups present in TCDs which is consistent with the FTIR and XPS results (Fig. S1 and Fig. S2). The narrow band gap of 10NFTCDs nanohybrid allowed the increased utilization of visible light irradiation which could ultimately led to the improved photocatalytic activities.

To get a deep insight of optical properties of the synthesized 10NFTCDs, their PL spectra were recorded at different range of λex (from 290 ̶ 470 nm) and the resultant spectra are presented in Fig. 5(b). It can be clearly seen that initially as the λex varied from 290 ̶ 320 nm the emission spectra of 10NFTCDs showed an increase in intensity, with λem maxima remained fixed at 430 nm. However, upon gradual increase of 10 nm in λex from 330 to 470 nm, the λem exhibited a slight red-shift accompanied by decrease in PL intensity. This excitation-dependent multicolor emission behavior could be attributed to the presence of distinct sizes of 10NFTCDs and dispersal of discrete emissive traps on the surface of each quantum dots [25,26]. Presence of different sizes of particles are consistent with the results of particle size distribution histogram constructed based on TEM results (Fig. 4j). Due to quantum confinement, increase in the particle sizes of 10NFTCDs cause a gradual decrease in energy. Consequently, presence of different sized 10NFTCDs gives rise to distinctive excitation-emission spectra. Furthermore, as suggested by XPS and FTIR results, the surface of 10NFTCDs exhibited a variety of functional groups, including COC, CO, and CO which resulted in series of emissive trap between π and π⁎ state of CC. When such surface is exposed to a specific λex, the respective emissive trap tends to overpower, subsequently bestowed disparate emission spectra. Thus, the excitation-dependent PL emission behavior of these 10NFTCDs could be attributed to both the presence of discrete sized quantum dots and different surface emissive traps. In addition, the effect of proportion of NF in the nanohybrid NFTCD was studied by recording PL intensity of xNFTCDs containing varying composition of NF (x = 5, 10, 15, and 20 wt% of NF). It can be seen from Fig. S5 that the PL intensity tends to decrease with increase in amount of NF due to increase in non-fluorescent content in the nanohybrid therefore a compromised ratio was chosen i.e., 10NFTCD in order to achieve sufficient magnetism without extensive loss of PL intensity.

The room temperature magnetic behavior of all the synthesized materials was accessed by VSM with an applied magnetic field ranging from −10,000 to +10,000 Oe as shown in Fig. 5(c). Pristine NF and 10NFTCDs nanohybrid exhibited S-shaped magnetic hysteresis (M-H) loop with specific saturation magnetization (Ms) of 60.63 emu/g and 12.28 emu/g, respectively which suggested their superparamagnetic behavior [2]. On contrary, pristine TCDs revealed its diamagnetic behavior with negative Ms value. Although the TCDs layers over NF NPs significantly reduced its Ms value, but the 10NFTCDs nanohybrid exhibited sufficient magnetization facilitating their recovery and recyclability through rapid magnetic separation using an external magnet rendering them economical material for sensing and removal purpose. Table S1 summarized the remanent magnetization (Mr) and magnetic coercivity (Hc) values calculated from magnified view of hysteresis loop of pristine NF and 10NFTCDs (inset of Fig. 5(c)).

Further, the bio-efficacy of the synthesized 10NFTCDs nanohybrid was surveyed through phytotoxicity assessment using Trigonella foenum-graecum seeds. Different seed germination parameters (GP and SVI) which quantify the phytotoxicity of 10NFTCDs nanohybrid were studied and compared with control experiment as shown in Table S2 [27,28]. The seed germination results obtained for 10NFTCDs were comparable with the control experiment. In fact, 10NFTCDs nanohybrid stimulated the seed growth, confirming their non-toxicity towards environment (Fig. 5(d)). This may be accredited to the biocompatible TCDs synthesized from green source and consequently these smart, biocompatible, magnetically retrievable and fluorescent photocatalyst can be safely used to safeguard the environment without imposing secondary pollutants into the ecosystem [29,30].

To evaluate the stability of fabricated 10NFTCDs nanohybrid, the effects of pH, salt concentration, photobleaching, and storage time was examined. As shown in Fig. S6, the PL intensity increases with decrease in pH from 7 to 2. This may be attributed to the higher net surface charge emerged due to immense protonation at lower pH which subsequently endowed the 10NFTCDs nanohybrid with reasonable hydrophilicity, stability and dispersibility in water and this ultimately kindle the strong PL intensity [20]. Moreover, the NF NPs tends to slightly solvate under acidic conditions thereby permitting more light to penetrate through the solution which eventually enhanced the PL intensity. Conversely, under alkaline conditions (pH >7), the surface becomes negatively charged, prompted the electrostatic repulsion leading to diminished PL intensity. Thus, NFTCDs nanohybrids possessed high potential for the application in waste-water sources, which usually possess acidic pH. Furthermore, salt study suggested the brilliant resistance of NFTCDs against extreme condition of ionic strength (up to 1 M concentration of NaCl) (Fig. S6). In addition, the PL intensity of NFTCDs remained constant under continuous lamp irradiation of 180 min or after storing 180 days (Fig. S6). These results manifested the excellent stability of NFTCDs which guarantees their profound applicability in real sample analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing strategy for the detection of NEs in aqueous system
ce:section.ce:para:
The excellent stability and fluorescence property of NFTCDs makes it imperative to explore their PL based sensing competence. Aromatic nitrophenols are the most commonly used chemicals by the industries; however, detection of these toxic NEs has drawn substantial attention owing to increasing concerns of homeland security, human health and environmental issues. Therefore, proficiency of the synthesized 10NFTCDs solution towards fluorescence sensing of aromatic nitrophenols based explosives was examined by comparing their performance with other aromatic compounds that have structural resemblance (including PNP, DNP, TNP, nitrobenzene (NB), 1,3-dinitrobenzene (DNB), phenol (PH), catechol (CT), toluene (TL), 4-nitrotoluene (PNT), 4-aminophenol (PAP)). For detection, PL emission spectra of NFTCDs solution (1 mg/mL) were recorded in the presence of 10 μM of above-mentioned aromatic compounds. From Fig. 6(a), it can be clearly seen that the quenching efficiency of NFTCDs nanohybrid towards PNP, DNP, and TNP was remarkably higher amongst other aromatic compounds, implying that the present fluorescent 10NFTCDs solution bears distinctly greater selectivity towards aromatic nitrophenols. Further, it is pertinent to note that many latent interferents may coexist in a practical environmental system. Thus, to check the detection selectivity of 10NFTCDs towards aromatic nitrophenols over other interferents, the competitive experiments were performed. Fig. 6(b) displayed that the PL quenching efficiency of 10NFTCDs towards aromatic nitrophenols (PNP, DNP, and TNP) in the presence of potential interferents. It was observed that PL intensity was comparable to that of the sole addition of respective nitrophenols, which imply that the developed NFTCDs based sensor procured renowned potential to detect the aromatic nitrophenols (PNP, DNP, and TNP) even in the presence of competing ion in a real environmental system. In order to evaluate the sensitivity of 10NFTCDs, concentration-gradient experiments towards PNP, DNP, and TNP were examined further. As revealed in Fig. 7 (a, c, e), the PL intensity of 10NFTCDs solution decreases gradually but at different degrees upon incremental addition of 0.1 mM solution of PNP, DNP and TNP. Moreover, upon increasing the concentration of NEs, the emission peak has undergone slightly red shift, this could be attributed to the hydrogen bond interaction and energy transfer mechanism occurring between 10NFTCDs and NEs. The changes of PL intensity were recorded based on the Stern-Volmer (SV) correlation, define by Eq. (3);
(3)
Io I
= 1 +
K SV
M

Where, I0 and I are the PL intensity before and after addition of the analyte, respectively, KSV is the quenching effect coefficient (M−1), and [M] is the molar concentration of the analyte [2,31]. As represented in inset of Fig. 7(b, d, f), the SV plot for all the nitrophenols (PNP, DNP, and TNP) were found to be almost linear at low concentration range (1–10 μM) and then deviates from linearity to exponential increase at higher concentration, which can be explained by self-adsorption or an energy-transfer process. To fit the nonlinear Stern-Volmer plot, an exponential Eq. (4) was applied to the system;
(4)
Io I
= a
exp
Vq
M
+ b

Where, a, b and Vq are constants. The data of PNP, DNP, and TNP illustrates that curves were perfectly fitted to exponential equation as shown in Fig. 7(b, d, f). The order of KSV values for the nitrophenols and the subsequent quenching efficiency was PNP > DNP > TNP with the KSV values 3.650 × 10−5 M−1, 2.520 × 10−5 M−1, and 1.810 × 10−5 M−1, respectively, which reveals a high quenching efficiency of aromatic nitrophenols on PL emission of NFTCDs. Based on the KSV values and standard deviation (σ), the limit of detection (LOD) for PNP, DNP, and TNP was estimated to be 65 nM, 74 nM, and 78 nM, respectively, using the ratio 3σ/KSV. Additionally, the performance of 10NFTCDs sensor was found similar or better in terms of LOD when compared with some nitro-explosive PL sensors reported in the literature (Table S3) and thus 10NFTCDs could be employed as a potential effective PL sensor for detection of nitro-explosive [32–39]. After the PL sensing experiments, the sensor was magnetically collected and then washed several times with ethanol followed by drying in oven. Notably, PL quenching efficiency of the 10NFTCDs sensor was essentially retained up to four cycles (Fig. S7) and therefore the sample could be regenerated and reused. These results recommended the good structural stability and reusability of the 10NFTCDs PL sensor after being utilized for NEs detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing strategy for the detection of MET antibiotic in aqueous system
ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics are frequently used in human and animal medicines to treat bacterial infections but nowadays these antibiotics released continuously into the aqueous system without rigorous obliteration, which became one of the serious organic pollutants owing to their long term persistency and uttermost toxicity. Therefore, efficient detection of antibiotics is of prime importance for the environmental safety and society welfare. Taking into consideration the extraordinary PL intensity of 10NFTCDs solution, it was explored for the trace level detection of antibiotics in aqueous samples. A total of seven frequently-used antibiotics were selected from different classes including MET, norfloxacin (NFL), amoxicillin (AMX), erythromycin (ERT), isoniazide (ISZ), levofloxacin (LVX), and sulfanilamide (SLM). To study the sensing performance, PL emission spectra of 10NFTCDs solution (1 mg/mL) was recorded and compared in the presence of 10 μM of different antibiotics. It was clearly observed from Fig. 8(a) that the presence of MET drastically quenched the PL intensity of 10NFTCDs solution. However, negligible variation ensues for the remaining antibiotics, indicating that 10NFTCDs is a proficient PL sensor for highly selective detection of MET antibiotic. On the basis of high quenching efficiency of 10NFTCDs towards MET, a competitive analyte test was performed in the presence of other potentially interfering antibiotics. As described in Fig. 8(b), upon addition of MET into NFTCDs solution in the presence of other antibiotics, the PL intensity was substantially quenched, proffering remarkable capability of the 10NFTCDs sensor for exclusively recognition of MET, even in the presence of an excess of other antibiotics. To further explore the quenching effect of MET on the PL emission of 10NFTCDs, similar PL titration experiments were performed (Fig. 8(c)) and the PL quenching efficiency was quantitatively described by the Stern-Volmer Eq. (3). The PL titration experiments revealed that the PL emission intensity rapidly died down upon incremental addition of MET solution and as indicated in Fig. 8(d), the SV plots for MET also exhibit two distinct regions: a good linear correlation at low concentration (0–50 μM), whereas a steep curve at high concentrations of MET. Effectiveness of the 10NFTCDs sensor towards detection of MET was also supported by high KSV value (2.185 × 10−5 M−1) and extremely low detection limit i.e., 57 nM, which was comparable to those of the well-designed luminescent sensors for MET reported in the literature (Table S4) [4,40–42]. In addition, recycling experiments have shown the 10NFTCDs PL sensor can be reused successfully up to four cycles (Fig. S7). All these results confirmed that 10NFTCDs PL sensor possessed good resistance against possible interferents and excellent recyclability for MET antibiotic sensing application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing strategy for the detection of DCNA pesticide in aqueous system
ce:section.ce:para: Pesticides are being enormously used in agriculture for protecting crops, however, their obnoxious effects have caused serious ecological problems and therefore their rapid and accurate detection is of foremost importance. Motivated by the tremendous PL sensing performance of 10NFTCDs towards aromatic nitrophenols and MET antibiotic, the sensor was further explored for the detection of pesticides. In this section seven commonly used pesticides namely, DCNA, methylparathion (MP), chlorpyrifos (CP), dicofol (DF), imidacloprid (IMD), glyphosate (GLP), and malathion (MT) were selected to examine the sensing effect of NFTCDs towards pesticides by adding 10 μM of pesticides into 1 mg/mL solution of 10NFTCDs. As evident from Fig. 9(a), the PL emission intensity of 10NFTCDs was spectacularly quenched by DCNA, meanwhile the other pesticides have no obvious effects on the PL intensity signifying the incredible selectivity of 10NFTCDs towards DCNA detection. In addition, the competition experiments unveiled the presence of other pesticides even at high concentration did not alter the PL quenching response induced by DCNA, which ensuring the anti-interference ability of the sensor (Fig. 9(b)). Quantification of PL quenching of 10NFTCDs solution by DCNA was achieved by using Stern-Volmer Eq. (3) (Fig. 9(c)). Here also, the non-linear SV plot at higher concentration of DCNA showed the existence of contemporaneous static and dynamic quenching and/or resonance energy transfer. Whereas, the KSV value (3.0201× 10−5 M−1) and LOD (100 nM) calculated from the linear fitting range of SV plot in lower concentration (0 ̶ 10 μM) was quite impressive (Fig. (9(d)) and analogous to the reported PL sensors used for the DCNA (Table S5) [2,43–45]. Recyclability of the sensor up to four times (Fig. S7) ultimately confirmed that the 10NFTCDs nanohybrid could act as a promising sensor for sensitive determination of DCNA pesticide.

For comprehensive waste water treatment there is need of designing a smart contaminant sensing-cum-annihilation system for the real-time detection and removal of pollutants (NEs, drugs and pesticides). While many reports have focused on PL based detection using CDs and photocatalytic degradation using NF NPs, but few reports were available on the nanohybrids with simultaneous detection and removal splendour. Herein, photocatalytic elimination ability of pristine TCDs, pristine NF NPs and xNFTCDs (x = 5, 10, 15, and 20 wt% of NF) nanohybrids were accessed by investigating their photocatalytic activity towards NEs (PNP, DNP, and TNP), drug (MET), and pesticide (DCNA) under visible light irradiation in the presence of H2O2 as oxidant. For all the contaminants, a constant decline in absorption maxima was observed in conjunction with the extension of visible light irradiation time which indemnify their complete degradation in that specific time interval.

Contribution of different reaction components such as presence of catalyst, H2O2, and light in the Fenton degradation process was appraised ahead of optimization studies. The photocatalytic degradation of NEs (PNP, DNP, and TNP), drug (MET) and pesticide (DCNA) was studied using 10NFTCDs as catalyst under different reaction conditions. The percentage of pollutant degraded (% degradation) was calculated using Eq. (5);
(5)
% Degradation =
Ao − At
Ao
× 100

Where, Ao and At represents the absorbance of pollutant solution at time = 0 and time = t, respectively. Fig. S8 displayed the % degradation vs time plot for the respective pollutant under following different sets of reaction conditions: (a) H2O2, (b) H2O2 + light, (c) 10NFTCDs + H2O2, (d) and 10NFTCDs + H2O2 + light. Initially, the blank experiments clearly indicated that none of the pollutant was able to undergo self-degradation undeterred by the presence of light and H2O2, thereby meaning that the pollutants cannot be degraded in the absence of catalyst. Further, with the addition of photocatalyst 10NFTCDs along with H2O2, the degradation efficiency was found to increase slightly, which could be attributed to the formation of Fenton's reagent. However, upon irradiating the light onto the reaction system containing the photocatalyst 10NFTCDs and H2O2, the degradation efficiency remarkably increased, indicating the necessity for photo-Fenton degradation of the pollutants, as the lamp irradiation can simulate the photocatalyst to promote photocatalysis [48].

Moreover, the effect of catalyst loading on the degradation efficiency was also studied to ameliorate the photodegradation reaction conditions. The amount of catalyst was varied from 0.25 g/L to 1.0 g/L in the presence of constant [H2O2] = 8.8 mM and pH = 2.5. Fig. S9 demonstrated that initially the degradation efficiency amplified with the increase in catalyst dosage up to 0.50 g/L which could be attributed to proliferation in number of catalytic sites present on the surface of catalyst. However, with further increase in the catalyst dosage, obscurity of the solution increased which obstruct the traverse of visible light and consequently decreased the photocatalytic efficiency [14]. Thus, 0.50 g/L concentration of the catalyst was selected for the subsequent photocatalytic studies.

The photocatalytic activity of all the synthesized samples was assessed towards the degradation of NEs (PNP, DNP, and TNP), drug (MET) and pesticide (DCNA) by photo-Fenton processes under the optimized conditions. Fig. S10 displayed the % degradation vs time plots for the photodegradation of selected pollutant using pristine NF, pristine TCD and xNFTCDs (x = 0, 5, 10, 15, and 20 wt% of NF) nanohybrids. It was observed that degradation was imperceptible when pristine TCDs were used as catalyst. On the other hand, pristine NF NPs showed photocatalytic capabilities to certain extent but inclusion of TCDs into the matrix of NF NPs, significantly improved the photo-Fenton degradation efficiency. All the nanohybrids exhibited improved photocatalytic activity in comparison to pristine TCDs. But, the photoactivity of NFTCDs system tends to decrease with increasing amount of NF above 10 wt%, this could be attributed to the aggregation of particles due to inherent magnetism of NF NPs. In addition, the degradation reactions were observed to follow pseudo first order kinetics and kinetic profile of individual degradation of pollutant were studied by means of the Langmuir-Hinshelwood kinetic model, according to which pseudo first order kinetics follows the relation given by Eq. (6);
(6)
ln
At
Ao
= − kt

Where, k is the pseudo first order rate constant [48]. Fig. 11 displays the plot of ln(At/Ao) vs time (min) and the rate constant (k) values calculated for degradation of each pollutant (PNP, DNP, TNP, MET and DCNA) have been summarized in Table 1 . The kinetic results also revealed that the incorporation of TCDs into the matrix of NF NPs dramatically enhanced the rate constant values for each pollutant studied, thereby indicating that the photocatalytic performance of xNFTCDs nanohybrids was superior to the pristine NF NPs. Moreover, the k values were observed to commensurate with the degradation curves (Fig. S10). In addition, FE-SEM and HR-TEM studies suggested that the close contact between the TCDs and NF subunits may trigger efficient electron transfer between the two components; which consequently, improved the charge separation and visible light photocatalytic activity. Thus, the improved catalytic efficiency of NFTCDs nanohybrids may be attributed to following reasons: (i) TCDs possess π-π conjugated structure due to which it can act as an electron acceptor, and when coalesced with NF NPs, form Ni/Fe ̶ O ̶ C coordination bonds that rapidly transfer photogenerated electrons from NF NPs to the TCDs, consequently, the severance efficiency of electron-hole pair and thus photocatalytic activities were extensively improved, (ii) TCDs contained a large number of oxygen containing hydrophilic functional groups which brings out more adsorption of H2O and H2O2 and thus increase their involvement in photo-Fenton reactions and, (iii) some of the additional photogenerated electron, involved in endorsement of Fe3+/Fe2+ cycle, directly reacts with H2O2 adsorbed on the surface of the TCDs to generate •OH, and subsequently boost-up the Fenton like activity such that it radically improved the photocatalytic degradation performance [12,49].

A general process for the degradation of noxious pollutants over NFTCDs under the combined action of H2O2 and visible light irradiation can be described by Eqs. (7 to 16). In photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants using photo-Fenton process, •OH is the major active species involved in the photocatalytic degradation process which can be produced in following two ways. Firstly, under light irradiation, NF surface gets excited to generate electron (e−)/hole (h+) pairs. Since TCDs were successfully applied on NF NPs to form Ni/Fe ̶ O ̶ C bonds, due to which the electrons were rapidly transferred between Ni/Fe centre and H2O2. The π-conjugated TCDs could act as photosensitizer owing to their broad visible light absorption capacity and donates e− to the CB of NF. The narrow band gap of NFTCDs nanohybrids and higher visible light absorption capacity as compared to pristine NF and pristine TCDs allowed higher production of e−/h+ pairs leading to higher photocatalytic ability. Meanwhile, the conducting texture of TCDs in the system also acted as an e− reservoir to trap the e− injected from CB of NF, thereby, TCDs receives a large amount of photogenerated electrons and reacts with the adsorbed H2O2 to form •OH thus inhibiting the recombination of e−/h+ pair and thereby improved the photocatalytic degradation efficiency (Eq. (7) and Eq. (8)) [48]. Secondly, it may be possible that some of the photogenerated electrons were excited from oxygen to the metal (O → Fe, OH → Fe) and by this means reduced Fe3+ to Fe2+ (Eq. (9)). In turn, Fe2+ 3d orbital and sp3 hybrid orbital of H2O2 adsorbed on the catalyst surface partially overlap, which debilitate the O ̶ O bond (Eq. (10)). This is conducive for the decomposition of H2O2 to generate a large amount of highly reactive •OH for the degradation of organic pollutants (Eq. (11)). Due to participation of photogenerated electrons, the consumption of H2O2 by the reduction of Fe3+ was decreased (Eq. (12) and Eq. (13)). The effective Fe3+/Fe2+ cycle ensured that enough Fe2+ participates in the photo-Fenton reaction (Eq. (9) and Eq. (10)) [12,49]. At the same time, some of the photogenerated electrons react with the dissolved O2 in the system to generate •O͞ 2, which participate in the degradation process (Eq. (14)). Although holes do not oxidise the organic pollutants, however, they react with H2O2 to form O2 and H+, which provides more O2 to form •O͞ 2 (Eq. (15));

The generated radicals •OH and •O͞ 2 can oxidise organic pollutants (PNP, DNP, TNP, MET and DCNA) as per the following reaction (Eq. (16));

Further, in order to investigate the photocatalytic activity of the catalyst for long term use, the catalyst 10NFTCDs was evaluated over four repeated degradation experiments. Owing to the magnetic nature of 10NFTCDs nanohybrid, it can be easily recovered using an external magnet, washed and dried in oven for further use. Fig. S11 showed that the 10NFTCDs photocatalyst maintains a high catalytic activity over a long period of use as no appreciable decrease in photocatalytic activity was observed up to four catalytic cycles. The treated solution after the degradation reaction was used to examine the metal ion (Fe and Ni) leaching, which is an important parameter to evaluate the stability of the catalyst during extremely acidic reaction conditions. For this Fe and Ni metal ion concentrations were measured before (initial pH = 7) and after the degradation reaction (first and fourth consecutive cycles, initial pH = 2.5). The results obtained from ICP-MS measurement (Table S6) established the stability of photocatalyst under extremely acidic conditions as the leaching concentrations of Fe and Ni ions in the treated water were almost negligible (as per the WHO standard for drinking water). In addition, the stability of the 10NFTCDs photocatalyst was further investigated by XPS, powder XRD, and FESEM analysis before and after the four consecutive photocatalytic cycles (Fig. S12 and Fig. S13). It can be noticed that no apparent changes were observed in the surface elemental composition, binding energies, crystallinity, and morphology of the catalyst, confirming the nanohybrid 10NFTCDs to be stable for four consecutive photocatalytic cycles. Hence, the catalyst can be practically used not only for PL sensing of organic pollutants (PNP, DNP, TNP, MET, and DCNA) but also for their photocatalytic degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical and photocatalytic performance of NFTCDs in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practicality of the developed method, water samples collected from Sukhna Lake, Chandigarh and Ganga River, Rishikesh were used to simulate the detection and degradation of selected contaminants in real samples. Prior to analysis, the samples (lake water and river water) were filtered to remove any particulate matter. As none of the contaminant (PNP, DNP, TNP, MET, and DCNA) was detected therefore, 0.50 μM of each selected contaminant were spiked individually into both the samples. For the detection applications, the solutions were further diluted to give two different concentrations (2 μM and 5 μM) of each selected contaminant. The established method was applied to analyze the spiked samples, with each analysis replicated thrice. As summarized in Table 2 , it can be seen that recoveries of NEs varied in the range of 99.38% - 100.73%, with relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 3) <3.5%. On the other hand, recoveries of MET and DCNA were in the range of 97.50% - 99.20% and 99.37% - 100.49% respectively, with RSD < 3%. Thus, the amount of each analyte determined using the proposed method was in good agreement with the spiked values. Additionally, the profound ability of the proposed method towards detection of MET antibiotic in real sample was recognized by validating the quantity of MET constituent present in the drug Rifaxigly-M purchased from the local market. Recovery of MET in drug was 100.61% with RSD below 3%. Thus, the experimental results established that the detection was not influenced by the constituents of the real samples and hence inveterate the feasibility and reliability of the 10NFTCDs based PL sensor for real-time monitoring of deadly chemicals in aqueous environment.

Furthermore, the photocatalytic efficacy of a photocatalyst can be assessed by studying its toxicity removal capability, which in turn can be measured in terms of phytotoxicity. As the photo-treated effluent would eventually merge with water resources, therefore phytotoxicity test was performed using seeds germination method to assess the toxicity of waste water and its sequential impact on the aquatic ecosystem [50]. After successful detection of contaminants in spiked river water, 100 mL of 0.50 μM contaminated river water was further employed as pilot samples. In this study, Trigonella foenum-graecum seeds were used to investigate the potentiality of 10NFTCDs to treat pilot samples under optimum conditions ([10NFTCDs] = 0.50 g/L, pH = 2.5, [H2O2] = 8.8 mM and 120 min Hg lamp irradiation]. Different parameters such as GP and SVI were calculated to quantify the phytotoxicity and results were summarized in Table 3 . The analyzed data revealed that the contaminated river water strongly inhibits the germination of Trigonella foenum-graecum seeds as compared to control sample (blank river water). However, the phytotoxicity level significantly reduced when the Trigonella foenum-graecum seeds were exposed to the recycled river water obtained after photo-treatment with 10NFTCDs (Fig. S14). Therefore, these results indicated that biocompatible 10NFTCDs nanohybrid possessed excellent photocatalytic degradation efficiency to reduce the phytotoxicity of wastewater containing pestiferous components.

In summary, a smart multifunctional platform based on NFTCDs nanohybrid was designed that exhibited excellent fluorescence property, water stability, and photocatalytic activity, for concomitant detection and degradation of NEs (PNP, DNP, and TNP), antibiotic (MET) and pesticide (DCNA). It has been found that NFTCDs demonstrated remarkable PL quenching efficiency and outstanding photocatalytic degradation ability towards virulent compounds (PNP, DNP, TNP, MET, and DCNA) as compared to pristine NF NPs. Moreover, NFTCDs showed extremely low detection limit towards each selected pollutant i.e., PNP (65 nM), DNP (74 nM), TNP (78 nM), MET (57 nM), and DCNA (1 μM). The high quenching efficiencies and prodigious photocatalytic activity can be attributed to the energy transfer between analyte-sensor system and synergic photo-Fenton mechanism, respectively. It has also been demonstrated that the catalytic activity played an important role in the enrichment of NEs, MET, and DCNA, which further increases the quenching efficiency of NFTCDs. Besides, the phytotoxicity assessment demonstrated the impressive photocatalytic efficacy of NFTCDs nanohybrid as the toxicity of photo-treated contaminated river water was significantly reduced as compared to untreated river water. Unlike the traditional materials, this systematically designed platform based on NFTCDs nanohybrid overcomes the huge energy gap to achieve two functions simultaneously and eventually opened a bright pathway to promote substantial advancement in the field of environmental monitoring and waste water management.
